1.synthesis: Paul closes the household code with the masters' corresponding duty: 'Masters, provide your slaves with what is right and fair, knowing that you also have a Master in heaven' (v.1). The single verse completes the structural pair of 3:22–25 (servants to masters) with its reciprocal (masters to servants). The ground is identical: masters have a Master. The heavenly lordship relativizes every earthly lordship from above, not by abolishing it but by holding it accountable to a higher standard of what is right and fair (to dikaion kai tēn isotēta — justice and equity).
1.historical_context: Gill notes the editorial awkwardness: this verse properly belongs to the preceding chapter, with which it should have been concluded, since it is strange that those who made the division of chapters and verses should separate this from the former chapter to which it so manifestly belongs. The master-servant relationship in the Roman world was characterized by near-absolute power — a Roman master could legally kill a slave without criminal consequence. Paul's instruction to provide what is right and fair is a significant qualification of that power. Matthew Henry observes that the motive given — knowing that you also have a Master in heaven — transforms the ethical obligation from social pressure to theological accountability: masters are not to be just because it is culturally expected but because they answer to a Lord who is just.
1.literary_structure: The verse serves as the hinge between the household code (3:18–4:1) and the community instructions (4:2–6). Structurally it completes the code and then releases it: the household relationships have been reconfigured by Christ's lordship; now the community's relationship to the outside world — prayer for Paul's mission, wise conduct toward outsiders, gracious speech — receives attention. The brevity of the master's instruction compared to the extended servant instruction of 3:22–25 may reflect the social composition of the community — more slaves than masters — but also the disproportionate accountability the greater power carries.
1.theological_application: The phrase right and fair (dikaion kai isotēs) is drawn from the language of Greek justice. Gill reads it as covering both the legal minimum and the equity beyond law: to give them the just due for their labour and services — what is according to law, right, and equity. The revolutionary character of the instruction lies not in its content — Roman moral philosophy also commended justice toward slaves — but in its ground: knowing that ye also have a Master in heaven. The master's accountability to the heavenly Master, who shows no partiality (3:25), creates an eschatological horizon for all earthly power. Every exercise of authority is an exercise conducted before the ultimate Authority, who will evaluate it with perfect equity.
1.christology: The Master in heaven to whom earthly masters are accountable is the Lord Christ of 3:24 — the same Lord who grants the inheritance that even the serving slave will receive. The ethical force of this statement for a slave-master: you are not the highest authority in your household. Your master is the one who died to reconcile all things to himself (1:20), who disarmed the powers (2:15), and who will appear in glory and bring those hidden with him into that glory (3:4). The ethics of the household are sustained by the eschatology of the letter: when Christ appears, every earthly distinction of master and slave will be reconfigured by the standard of the heavenly Master's justice and equity.

2.synthesis: Paul turns to the community's common life beyond the household: Continue steadfastly in prayer, being watchful in it with thanksgiving (v.2). He asks them to pray for him too — that God would open a door for the word, to declare the mystery of Christ for which he is in prison, that he might make it clear as he ought to speak (vv.3–4). Walk in wisdom toward outsiders, making the best use of time (v.5). Let speech always be gracious, seasoned with salt — knowing how to answer each person (v.6).
2.historical_context: Gill identifies the prayer of v.2 as prayer to God, for though the object is not expressed here, he is named in the following verse. The instruction to watch in prayer (grēgorountes) has the sense of staying alert, not drifting into mechanical repetition — the same word used for watchmen on the city walls. The open door for the word Paul requests (v.3) is a standard Pauline metaphor for missionary opportunity (1 Corinthians 16:9; 2 Corinthians 2:12). Chrysostom applies the wise conduct toward outsiders to the apostolic model: what Christ said to his disciples — be wise as serpents and harmless as doves — Paul now advises the Colossians themselves.
2.literary_structure: The section has two movements: the community's prayer posture (vv.2–4) and its engagement with outsiders (vv.5–6). The center of the prayer appeal is Paul's own missionary situation — prayer is not abstract but specific and urgent. The mystery of Christ that Paul wants to proclaim clearly is the same mystery announced in 1:27 — Christ in you, the hope of glory — making the community's prayer a direct contribution to the ongoing apostolic proclamation of the letter's central christological claim. The gracious speech seasoned with salt of v.6 closes the section: the community's outward-facing conversations should be characterized by both attractiveness (grace) and a preserving, purifying edge (salt).
2.theological_application: Gill on continue in prayer reads the exhortation as calling not to occasional or formulaic prayer but persistent, persevering, and attentive prayer: he would have them be diligent and constant in it, and not leave it off, to persevere in it and be instant therein. The thanksgiving that accompanies prayer is not a separate act but its constant accompaniment — prayer made with thanksgiving is prayer that recognizes what God has already done as the ground for asking what remains to be done. Gill on gracious speech (v.6) expands: let grace be the subject matter of your speech and conversation — when saints meet together they should converse about the work of grace upon their souls, and confer together about the doctrines of grace. Gracious speech is not merely pleasant speech but speech whose content is the grace of God.
2.christology: The mystery of Christ that Paul asks prayer to open doors for (v.3) is Christ himself — not doctrines about him abstractly but Christ as the content of the proclaimed word. The mystery is Christ in you, the hope of glory (1:27), the news that God's hidden plan for the ages has been disclosed in a person who indwells his people. The door for the word is therefore a door for Christ — the proclamation that opens is an opening through which Christ enters into communities that receive the word. Paul in prison, asking for prayer that he might make the mystery clear, is himself an enactment of the letter's theology: the power that opens doors works through the weakness of a prisoner, because in him all the fullness of deity dwells bodily and his imprisoned ambassador speaks with his authority.

7.synthesis: Paul closes with a network of personal greetings that give the abstract theology of the letter a human face. Tychicus, a beloved brother and faithful minister and fellow servant in the Lord, is sent to bring news of Paul and encourage their hearts (vv.7–8). He comes with Onesimus, the faithful and beloved brother who is one of them (v.9). Greetings from Aristarchus, Mark (Barnabas's cousin), and Jesus called Justus — these three are the only Jewish believers among his co-workers for the kingdom of God, and they have been a comfort to him (vv.10–11). Epaphras, their own member, greets them — always struggling in prayer that they may stand mature and fully assured in all the will of God (v.12). Luke the beloved physician and Demas also greet them. Greetings to Nympha and the church in her house; greetings to Laodicea; they are to exchange letters with the Laodicean church. And: tell Archippus, see that you fulfill the ministry that you have received in the Lord. Paul's closing benediction in his own hand: Remember my chains. Grace be with you (v.18).
7.historical_context: Gill notes that Tychicus was an Asian by birth and was employed by the apostle as a messenger to Colossae, as he also was to Ephesus, to inform the members of the church of his state and condition — that he was in prison at Rome. The mention of Onesimus — one of you — is remarkable: the runaway slave now returning to Colossae is described as faithful and beloved brother, placing him in the same relational category as Epaphras and the other named co-workers. This is the social reality of neither slave nor free (3:11) made concrete. Epaphras's prayers for the Colossians to stand mature and fully assured in all the will of God (v.12) echo the letter's opening prayer (1:9) and its goal (1:28), suggesting Epaphras has internalized the letter's concerns for his community.
7.literary_structure: The closing section performs the theology of the letter: the community it describes is racially mixed (Jews and Gentiles), socially mixed (free and enslaved, rich householders and itinerant ministers), geographically connected (Colossae, Laodicea, Rome), and entirely organized around mutual service in the Lord and for the kingdom of God. The exchange of letters with Laodicea (v.16) shows that Paul's theological response to the Colossian error was intended to circulate beyond its immediate recipients. The brief instruction to Archippus (v.17) is a reminder that the letter's audience includes not merely the congregation but specific individuals within it who carry named responsibilities. The final remember my chains before the grace benediction brings the entire abstract theology home to a concrete human situation: Paul sits in prison, and his grace-wish for them is made from that place of weakness.
7.theological_application: Gill on grace be with you reads the benediction as a prayer: the grace of God, the Father of Christ, or rather the grace of Christ himself, which is manifold, large, and rich — including all the blessings of grace, righteousness, peace, pardon, and eternal life. The final invocation of grace returns the letter to its opening: grace to you and peace from God our Father (1:2). The network of personal greetings is itself a practical instantiation of the theological claim that in Christ there is no Greek or Jew, slave or free: the letter closes by naming a Jewish prisoner, a Gentile physician, a converted slave, a female householder, and a co-worker in the same breath as partners in the same apostolic enterprise. The grace-wish that closes is the entire theology of the letter compressed into two words: all that Paul has argued about Christ — his fullness, his lordship, his reconciling work, his indwelling presence — is what grace carries.
7.christology: Remember my chains (v.18) is the letter's last christological statement before the benediction. The chains are Paul's for the mystery of Christ (v.3) — he is imprisoned for the proclamation of the very Christ whose supremacy the letter has argued. His imprisonment is itself an enactment of 1:24: I rejoice in my sufferings for your sake, and in my flesh I am filling up what is lacking in Christ's afflictions. The Christ who is all in all (3:11) is the Christ whose ambassador suffers in chains, and the grace-wish Paul sends is the grace of that same Christ — inexhaustible, not diminished by the prison, flowing from the one in whom all fullness dwells bodily.
